annotations.text: I suspect that transient tachypnea of the newborn prompted the initial x-ray film of the chest, which demonstrated the mass.
I shall therefore focus on neurogenic tumors of the posterior mediastinum as the primary neoplastic consideration in this infant.
The presence of calcific densities within a posterior mediastinal lesion is highly suggestive of a neural-crest tumor.

the cleavage plane between the paraspinal tissues and the mass, the absence of spinal involvement, the absence of calcification, and the lack of evidence of distortion of adjacent structures such as the ribs, blood vessels, and airways make the diagnosis of a neural-crest tumor unlikely in this case from a radiographic standpoint.
I agree with them, although I would have needed the added comfort provided by a negative assay of urine for catecholamines.

For a diagnosis in this case I would have obtained an assay for urinary catecholamine values and additional imaging to delineate the vascular features, and I would have proceeded with an exploratory thoracotomy.

I believed that statistically the diagnosis was most likely to be a left-lower-lobe pulmonary sequestration, but I was troubled by the observations of agitation and bradycardia (80 beats per minute) — an unusual combination.
If there had been air it would have been easy to detect it on the CT scan.
The combination of diagnostic information, prevention of future infections, prevention of spontaneous bleeding, and allowance for unimpeded lung growth produces a compelling argument for removal.
Sequestered lobes tend to become infected and may be complicated by abscess formation.
The observation of lymphangiectasia in this case suggests such a mechanism.
